impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of its unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act when the unpatented products have substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act for conditioning licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' exclusive purchase of Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the jury's finding and corresponding damages award, affirming that the tied products had substantial non-infringing uses beyond the patent's scope.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors harmed by patent-based tying arrangements have a clear path to antitrust damages where the tied product has substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face significant antitrust liability risk if they condition patent licenses on exclusive purchase of unpatented products, even if the products are related to the patented technology. Companies must carefully structure licensing to avoid tying claims.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to methods for detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls in an emulator to a model of expected function calls are abstract and ineligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million infringement judgment, holding that Columbia's cybersecurity patent claims for detecting anomalous program executions were abstract at step one of the Alice framework. The court also reversed the district court's denial of JMOL on damages for foreign sales and vacated enhanced damages and attorneys' fees awards that had been based partly on an improper contempt finding against opposing counsel.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies facing patent infringement claims for software-based inventions have a stronger basis to challenge eligibility under § 101, particularly for claims directed to comparing data against models to detect anomalies. The ruling also limits damages based on foreign sales.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in cybersecurity and software fields face heightened risk that their claims will be found abstract under Alice, even after surviving earlier eligibility challenges and obtaining jury verdicts.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to a server that tailors content presentation specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities before delivering the content are ineligible for patenting under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held on cross-appeal that GoTV's streaming media patents claiming server-side tailoring of content specifications to device capabilities were ineligible under § 101, reversing the district court's judgment and entering judgment for Netflix. The court reversed the finding of indefiniteness on the '865 patent but found all asserted claims patent-ineligible.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming service providers and technology companies benefit from a strengthened § 101 defense against patent claims covering adaptive content delivery to devices with varying capabilities.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims directed to server-side content adaptation and delivery optimization face increased invalidity risk under § 101, even where claims include specific technical implementation details.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims for a web conferencing system with time-shifting capabilities enabling participants to view sessions asynchronously at different playback rates are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's dismissal under Rule 12(b)(6) for failure to state a claim, finding that the '637 patent's web conferencing claims with time-shifting and asynchronous viewing capabilities were ineligible under § 101. This represents another significant § 101 ruling invalidating software-related patents at the pleading stage.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies can challenge patent eligibility at the motion to dismiss stage, avoiding costly discovery and trial on claims directed to conventional conferencing features.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders asserting software and communication method patents face the risk of early dismissal on § 101 grounds before any discovery or claim construction occurs.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in obtaining patents and on a Walker Process antitrust fraud claim alleging attempted monopolization through enforcement of a fraudulently obtained patent.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgments on both inequitable conduct (granted in favor of Global Tubing) and Walker Process fraud (granted in favor of Tenaris), finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. The case involves coiled tubing patents where Tenaris allegedly failed to disclose prior art CYMAX documents during prosecution.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies accused of inequitable conduct benefit from a higher evidentiary bar requiring trial on disputed facts rather than summary disposition. Similarly, accused infringers pursuing Walker Process antitrust claims can survive summary judgment with sufficient evidence of fraud.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face continued exposure to both inequitable conduct defenses and Walker Process antitrust claims where prior art disclosure issues exist, as courts cannot resolve factual disputes on summary judgment.

impacts.caseName: REGENXBIO Inc. v. Sarepta Therapeutics, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether claims directed to cultured host cells containing a recombinant nucleic acid molecule encoding a specific AAV capsid protein with a heterologous non-AAV sequence are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101 or directed to a natural phenomenon.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's grant of summary judgment that REGENXBIO's gene therapy patent claims were ineligible under § 101, holding that claims to engineered host cells containing recombinant nucleic acid molecules encoding specific AAV capsid proteins with heterologous sequences are not directed to a natural phenomenon. The court remanded for further proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Biotech and pharmaceutical companies receive important protection for gene therapy inventions, as engineered host cells with recombinant constructs are patent-eligible subject matter even when based on naturally occurring viral sequences.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies seeking to develop competing gene therapy products using similar AAV vectors face continued patent barriers, as eligibility challenges under § 101 are less likely to succeed against claims to engineered biological constructs.

impacts.caseName: Apple Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple's importation and sale of Apple Watch models with blood oxygen measurement features violated Section 337 of the Tariff Act by infringing Masimo's patents on wearable pulse oximetry devices.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated Section 337 by importing Apple Watches with blood oxygen features that infringed Masimo's patents, upholding the limited exclusion order barring importation of infringing models. The court found no error in the ITC's domestic industry determination, validity rulings, or infringement findings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in wearable health technology have a validated path to ITC exclusion orders against major technology companies importing infringing products, providing powerful enforcement leverage.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies incorporating health-monitoring features into consumer devices face significant import exclusion risks where those features are covered by existing patents, potentially requiring product redesigns or licensing agreements.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a respondent in an ITC proceeding that missed the statutory deadline to seek a mandatory stay of a district court action under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) may obtain such a stay by voluntarily dismissing and refiling its declaratory judgment action.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that an ITC respondent cannot circumvent the 30-day deadline for seeking a mandatory stay under § 1659(a)(2) by dismissing and refiling its district court declaratory judgment action. The court rejected BioMarin's attempt to reset the statutory clock, preserving Ascendis's ability to litigate its safe harbor defense in district court while the ITC investigation continued.
impacts.positiveImplications: Parties asserting declaratory judgment claims maintain their right to proceed in district court when ITC respondents miss the statutory stay deadline, preventing strategic manipulation of parallel proceedings.
impacts.negativeImplications: ITC respondents must strictly comply with the 30-day deadline to seek mandatory stays of parallel district court actions; the deadline cannot be reset through voluntary dismissal and refiling tactics.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent damages expert report and granting summary judgment for absence of a remedy based on that exclusion.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report and vacated summary judgment in favor of Microsoft on network optimization patents. The court found the district court abused its discretion in its Daubert ruling, reinstating the patent infringement case involving Azure SmartNIC technology.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs benefit from a more favorable standard for admission of damages expert testimony, making it harder for defendants to obtain summary judgment solely by excluding damages evidence.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies defending patent suits face reduced ability to dispose of cases at summary judgment through Daubert challenges to damages experts, potentially increasing litigation costs.

impacts.caseName: National Veterans Legal Services Program v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users by using fees to fund expenses unrelated to PACER operations, and whether incentive awards to class representatives were permissible.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's approval of a $125 million settlement in a nationwide class action challenging excessive PACER fees, finding the settlement fair and the attorneys' fees and incentive awards to nonprofit class representatives proper. The court rejected objections regarding subject matter jurisdiction, settlement fairness, and the permissibility of incentive awards.
impacts.positiveImplications: The decision validates incentive awards for class representatives and provides a framework for settling government overcharge class actions, benefiting businesses and organizations that frequently access court records.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Board governing discretionary denial of inter partes review petitions constitute 'general statements of policy' exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's ruling that the PTO Director's instructions governing discretionary IPR institution decisions are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements. The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution and that the instructions do not bind the Director.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's continued flexibility to exercise discretion in declining to institute IPR proceedings, preserving patent stability.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies seeking to challenge patents through IPR face continued uncertainty from the PTO's discretionary institution framework, which is insulated from both judicial review and formal rulemaking requirements.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding expert testimony on induced infringement of spinal surgery device patents and in granting JMOL to the defendant.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of two expert witnesses and grant of JMOL to DePuy in a patent case involving spinal derotation surgical tools, remanding for a new trial. The court found the district court abused its discretion in its Daubert rulings on both technical and damages experts.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs benefit from a more protective appellate standard against overly aggressive Daubert exclusions in patent cases involving medical device technology.
impacts.negativeImplications: Medical device companies face renewed exposure to patent infringement claims after expert exclusion rulings are reversed, increasing litigation costs and potential damages exposure.

impacts.caseName: Crocs, Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC properly entered only a limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents rather than a general exclusion order in a Section 337 trademark investigation, and whether the appeal of the no-violation finding as to active respondents was timely.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit dismissed Crocs's appeal of the ITC's no-violation finding against active respondents as untimely and affirmed the ITC's entry of a limited (rather than general) exclusion order against defaulting respondents in a trademark case involving Crocs's 3D trade dress marks for its Classic Clog shoes.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers of competing products benefit from the ITC's application of standard likelihood-of-confusion and dilution analyses in trade dress cases, as well as strict enforcement of appeal deadlines.
impacts.negativeImplications: Brand owners seeking broad import exclusion through ITC proceedings must strictly comply with appeal deadlines and recognize that default orders against some respondents do not automatically extend to general exclusion orders covering all potential infringers.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether claims of a crop harvester height-control patent containing means-plus-function limitations were indefinite due to insufficient algorithmic disclosure of the corresponding structure for the 'control means' limitation.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's finding that asserted claims of a crop harvester patent were invalid as indefinite, holding that the specification's disclosure of a 'controller interface,' 'head controller,' and 'hydraulic control system' provided sufficient corresponding structure for the 'control means' limitation even without detailed algorithmic disclosure.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with means-plus-function claims benefit from a less restrictive standard for corresponding structure disclosure, particularly for analog control systems where detailed algorithms may not be required.
impacts.negativeImplications: Agricultural equipment manufacturers and other accused infringers face reduced ability to invalidate means-plus-function claims for indefiniteness where the specification identifies conventional components as corresponding structure.
